title: SEGMENT 1 — The central pharmacology — focused delivery

lines.text: SEGMENT 1 — The central pharmacology — focused delivery
Alright, let's get started.
Today we're going to spend two hours on one of the strangest, most beautiful, and most commercially explosive drug classes ever invented — antibody-drug conjugates, or ADCs.
And I want to begin not with the molecule, but with the problem they were invented to solve, because that problem is older than oncology itself.
It's older than chemistry as we know it.
It's a problem that has haunted every doctor who has ever tried to kill something inside a patient without killing the patient.
The problem is this.
Cancer cells are us.
They are our own cells, with our own DNA, our own membranes, our own metabolism, gone rogue.
The differences between a cancer cell and a healthy cell are real, but they are subtle — a few mutated genes, a few overexpressed proteins, some altered surface markers.
From a chemical standpoint, a breast cancer cell and a normal breast cell are about as similar as two leaves from the same tree.
So when you try to poison the cancer, you almost inevitably poison the rest of the body too.
The patient loses their hair, their immune system collapses, they vomit for weeks, their gut lining sloughs off.
Sometimes the chemotherapy kills them faster than the tumor would have.
We accept this because we have no choice.
Or — until recently — we had no choice.
Here's the analogy I want you to hold in your head for the next two hours.
Imagine you are a general trying to bomb an enemy command bunker that happens to be located in the middle of a friendly city.
You have two options.
Option one: you carpet-bomb the entire city.
You kill the enemy, but you also kill thousands of civilians, you flatten hospitals, you destroy infrastructure.
This is traditional chemotherapy.

You spray cytotoxic poison through the entire bloodstream, hoping the rapidly dividing cancer cells will die a little faster than the rapidly dividing cells in your hair follicles, your bone marrow, and your gut lining.
Sometimes they do.
Often they don't, fast enough.
Option two: you build a guided missile.

A missile with a targeting computer that recognizes the enemy bunker — by its shape, by its radio signature, by something unique about it — and flies through the city without exploding until it reaches the exact target.
Then, and only then, it detonates.
The civilians live.
The bunker dies.
That is an antibody-drug conjugate.
It is a guided missile for cancer.
The targeting computer is an antibody — a Y-shaped protein, exactly like the antibodies your immune system makes, engineered to recognize a protein that sits on the surface of cancer cells.
The warhead is a chemotherapy drug so toxic that you could never inject it directly into a patient — it would kill them at doses ten thousand times below what you'd need to treat a tumor.
The thing that holds the warhead onto the missile until it arrives — that's the linker, a stretch of chemistry that releases the payload only at the right moment, in the right place.
That's it.
That's the whole concept.
Antibody plus linker plus payload.
A guided missile carrying a poison too dangerous to use any other way, delivered selectively to cells that bear the right surface marker.

Everything we'll talk about today — the chemistry of the linkers, the classes of payloads, the math of how many warheads you can hang off one antibody, the bystander effect, the lung toxicity, the manufacturing nightmares — all of it hangs off that one central idea: focused delivery of a poison too toxic to use without focusing it.

If you walk out of here in two hours and you can do four things — one, sketch an ADC and tell me what each of its three components does and why; two, explain in plain English why T-DXd is rewriting the textbook on HER2-low breast cancer when T-DM1 couldn't; three, look at a new ADC in clinical trials and tell me what its target is, what payload class it carries, and roughly where it will fail; four, explain what the bystander effect is and why it's both a superpower and a source of toxicity —
then we've succeeded.
Everything else hangs off those four.
A quick scale-setting before we go further.
Trastuzumab deruxtecan — the drug we'll call T-DXd or by its brand name Enhertu — was approved in 2019 and by 2024 it was generating over five billion dollars a year in revenue.
Five billion.
From one drug.
It is rewriting the entire approach to HER2-low breast cancer, which is a patient population we previously thought was beyond the reach of HER2-targeted therapy at all.

There are now over a dozen approved ADCs, hundreds in clinical development, and the field is producing more startup activity, more acquisitions, and more frontier chemistry than almost any other area of drug development.
We are living through the ADC moment.
By the end of this lecture, you'll understand why.
